q: The Doppler effect describes a change in the amplitude of a wave due to relative motion between source and observer.
The Doppler effect was first described by Isaac Newton in the 17th century.
The Doppler effect typically involves the motion of the source and observer directly perpendicular to each other.
A common example of the Doppler shift is the change of pitch heard when a vehicle's horn approaches and recedes from an observer.
In the Doppler effect, if the observer is stationary and the source is moving, no frequency shift will occur.
The Doppler equation $f_o = f_s \left( \frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s} \right)$ applies only when both the source and observer are moving.
In the Doppler equation, $f_o$ represents the frequency produced by the source.
When the source and observer are approaching each other, the observed frequency ($f_o$) is less than the source frequency ($f_s$).
A positive sign for $v_o$ (observer speed) is used in the Doppler equation when the observer moves away from the source.
If the source moves towards the observer, a negative sign is used for $v_s$ (source speed) in the denominator of the Doppler equation.
When both the observer and source are stationary, the Doppler effect causes a significant frequency shift.
Red shift is a phenomenon where the wavelength of light from a star shifts towards the blue end of the spectrum when the Earth and the star move away from each other.
Red shift is used to determine the velocity of astronomical bodies moving away from the Earth.
Radar uses sound waves to measure the velocity of detected objects.
The Doppler effect is utilized in police radar to measure the speed of vehicles.
Doppler ultrasound is a medical technique primarily used to image bone structures.
Angiodynography is a term used to describe measuring Doppler shift in frequency of ultrasound reflected by red blood cells.
Doppler ultrasound can help reveal blockages to blood flow and the formation of plaque in arteries.
If a star is approaching Earth, its spectral lines would shift towards the red end of the spectrum.
The Doppler equation accounts for the speed of sound ($v$) in the medium.
The Doppler effect occurs only with sound waves, not electromagnetic radiation.
The term 'Doppler shift' is an alternative name for the Doppler effect.
If the observer is moving away from the source, a negative sign is used for $v_o$ in the Doppler equation.
A positive sign for $v_s$ (source speed) is used when the source moves towards the observer.
Weather radar is an application of the Doppler effect.
Doppler ultrasound can provide detailed information on the heartbeat of a fetus during labor and delivery.
The Doppler effect is observable only when the source and observer are moving at the same speed.
When the source and observer are receding from each other, the observed frequency ($f_o$) is greater than the source frequency ($f_s$).
Radar relies on the principle of echolocation, similar to how sound-based echolocation works.
The speed of the wave medium is assumed to be in motion relative to the source and observer when considering the Doppler effect.
Red shift refers to a decrease in wavelength when a star moves towards Earth.
Doppler ultrasound is used to examine organs such as the heart and kidney.
The speed of the observer ($v_o$) is always subtracted in the numerator of the Doppler equation.
The Doppler effect is applicable to both sound waves and light waves.
The fractional change in wavelength ($\frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda}$) can be used to determine the velocity of an astronomical body moving away from the Earth.
Air traffic control radar is an application of the Doppler effect.
In the Doppler equation, $v_s$ represents the speed of sound in the medium.
When a source of sound is moving away from a stationary observer, the observed frequency will increase.
The Doppler effect only applies to phenomena in a vacuum, not in media like air or water.
Blue shift occurs when an astronomical body is moving towards the Earth.
The Doppler effect is important for distinguishing between pitch and loudness of sound.
